Let me preface this by saying that I'm not particularly picky about this sort of thing. My fireplace is literally a hole in the wall with some bricks in the back and a chimney on top. We're not talking Ethan Allen massive glass-fronted gold-plated blah, blah—I just need something to burn.

You know, make fire.

Now if you're like me, you don't make fire to heat your house. It's purely cosmetic. It's nice to have something "warm and cozy" going over in the corner on a special occasion. So I bought a six-pack of these logs instead of the usual logs I buy because the price was right. Oops.

Here's the thing. These aren't made of sawdust bound with wax like the others. They are made of trash bound with wax. As in, boxes, newspapers, wrappers, whatever. Coated in wax and pushed together into a loggish sort of thing.

This doesn't immediately set off alarm bells. Sounds sensible, right? What could be more "green" than recycling? Reduce, reuse, recycle, right? But there are problems. Multiple problems. Serious problems.

1) They just plain don't start. You'll light the wrapper and it will burn away and then you'll have a pile of trash sitting in your fireplace. Like, it literally looks like someone's trash. Pros:
- Correct size and Thread to replace the defective breakers on the hose cocks.
- Material Quality was the same as the OEM parts.
- Easy to install if you do not destroy the threads on the hose cock when removing the vacuum breaker.
- Comes with rubber gasket so no other parts needed to install.
- Very fast delivery.

Cons:
- While less expensive than the OEM parts, still somewhat pricey for a basic turned metal piece. The price was of this part was the lowest I could find so it is really not a negative.

I purchased a building that had the hose cocks with external vacuum breakers which were broken and leaking. Replacement vacuum breakers are expensive, prone to failure, and have a tendency to leak. I took the defective vacuum breakers off and replaced them with these pass through adapters. The water flow rate is much improved and there are no leaks to waste water.

Be sure to choose the correct size adapter to fit the thread size and pattern on your hose cock. Also, some vacuum breakers are pinned or have screws attaching the vacuum breaker to the hose cock which need to be removed before the vacuum breaker can be removed. If you just try to unscrew those from the hose cock you can destroy the threads or destroy the hose cock entirely.
top_profane.title: JL Brand SM01 Vacuum Breaker Adapter - Same Product Just Less Expensive

I am SO done with Hayward. I bought my 2nd Hayward filter 1 year ago via Amazon, and while I had some minor concerns over my first one, overall it kept my pool clean. I bought a second because we moved and installed a pool at the new house. Now the bad stuff....
The lint trap on the first one was so tight to close, that I risked cracking the lid every time I tried to open it. Problem solved on the new filter. But ..
The shut off valve that comes with the filter is the same crappy, ungodley expensive ball valve that they supplied on the old one. This valve will fail or crack within a few months so don't even bother using it. Get a gate valve instead and throw this pos that Hayward wants $35 for a replacement, right in the trash.
2nd filter came with 2 hoses. While I didn't expect this, one of them leaked right from the start. My option was to send the whole filter back to Amazon and order another, or buy a replacement hose locally. I chose the latter. Hayward only supplies ONE valve, the crappy ball valve described above. Apparently they think you should choose between whether you want your pool area flooded when you clean out the hair and lint trap, OR have your pool area flooded when you drain the filter. With one valve, you cannot protect both operations. Of course with the crappy valve they do furnish, you'll be flooding any way you look at it.

This appears to have done the trick. You have to know a few things about ghost ants. They're greedy, horny, snobby little bastards. The ones you see- they're all female, the males only live a week, and only live to impregnate the females. The females live a very long time, they live to breed, and when different colonies meet- unlike other ants that wipe each other out- ghost ants hook up, share males, and get to partying *together.* Which means an infestation gets out of hand really fast, as it has in my house. All exterior walls, every hole 1mm in diameter, these greedy little bastards moved in. I tried every granule poison Home Depot supplies, nothing. I tried baking soda with sugar, it killed a few, but the majority weren't interested, they certainly didn't carry it back to their friends. Petulant, greedy, bastards. A professional service came out and put down Terro Boric Acid, and that seemed to work, but my infestation was too large (read: expensive) for the amount of work required.

So I bought this stuff, and spent 2 hours working around my house, putting tiny little drops (they seem to like tiny drops better than big pools) everywhere along the hundreds of pheromone trails, and squeezing a little droplet into each opening in the brickwork that they were pouring out of. As you can tell from my phone pictures, they were drawn to it like a Hollywood ac
top_profane.title: Fast-Acting Ghost Ant Cocaine

top_profane.text:
ANT PSYCHOLOGY 101: Apartments

Q: How do you remove ants in a four unit building?
A: Study your enemy, learn how it thinks.

If you have to sum up ants in one word, greed. It's their greatest strength, and their greatest weakness.

Day One: Put two Terro baits in the apartment. One in the kitchen, one in the bathroom, as this was where they were active. Bastards were actually attracted to my sink drains. Keeping a clean home means nothing when the rotten bastards are interested in consuming tap water and fermenting hair in the sink drain.

Day Five: I have conquered most of their forces, yet the enemy continues its tiny assaults in small platoons. I was sitting around wondering why my Terro experience wasn't as awesome as all the other reviews. It's because the bastard ants had other supply trains in the other apartment units. Well then... time to exploit their weakness...

Day Six: I set out four new terro baits in each DMZ. Some bastard ant platoon must have blitzed in their ant pants when they discovered this cornucopia of delicious Borax booze. They chose to party in the kitchen, sending all of their units and apparently having a typical Roman Saturnalia next to my sink.

Day Seven: Bastards are dead. Now I can leave my dishes out after dinner and be lazy about washing them until the morning!
top_profane.title: It's their greatest strength, and their greatest weakness

top_profane.text:
These devices will make using garden hoses much less of a hassle. Connecting hoses to sill-cocks, to other hoses, to nozzles, or to sprinklers will be quicker and more secure. There are plastic versions available, but I'd suggest the brass. You know what you have to connect, so you can figure the number of fittings in both pairs and singly. If your sill cocks are close to the ground, you may want to increase convenience by getting swivel gooseneck fittings -- also Amazon items -- but if you live in a frost-prone area, make sure the goosenecks are open. Even if you have frost-proof sill-cocks, there can still be freezing issues unless you use the open goosenecks.
I'd recommend keeping the fittings mud/dirt free. I'd also recommend shutting off the sill-cock and opening the nozzle before removing the fitting from the sill-cock.
Yes, there is one more recommendation: Always leave the hoses empty. Not only are they easier to roll up, but there's no chance of the breaks that can happen in charged hoses in the sun.
top_profane.title: Hose-Hassle Reducers.

top_rant.text:
UPDATE after three months:

It's not good as an ups. Or backup power when connected to mains. This won't be addressed as there are no firmwares or anything to update.

After a few month's, and a few power outages, and after already realizing it doesn't automatically turn back on, I kept it. However the UPS function has more flaws. It fails under a typical brownout. So any short brownout or surge, may not reliably keep power going. It may turn off. I just don't think it's correct to market any UPS functionality. Yes, it can "work". In a lab maybe. But under real world conditions it does not.

Examples:
Long power outage: AC loss, Battery drains completely, that's fine, but when AC recovers, the inverter will not restore power. So you'd have to be there to turn it back on once it reaches 2% charge.

Medium clean power outage: If AC power is removed, the anker will carry the load, and when AC cleanly recovers, it'll passthrough and charge. My computers handle this fine. This is the only scenario that works how an ups should work.

Brownouts: Apparently under these conditions, there is no hysteresis, and the anker will just turn off. If power comes back on too quickly?, it'll turn off. A brownout happened while you were gone? Better make sure your fridge isn't off. You had a brownout while sleeping? I guess yo
top_rant.title: I kept it, despite some flaws, now I regret my use case (UPS) and the money

Spent the whole summer fighting the dread E90 (low water flow) error. Usually this was indicative of clogged filters but then I was getting it every day or two even with a brand new set of filters. By the end of the summer started getting an indication of the real problem - small pools of water under the filter pointed toward a leak somewhere in the filter system. This was confirmed by bleeding the air from the pump frequently, which kept the filter running longer. Still as time wore on, it got to the point where the filter would not stay running but a few hours (or even minutes!).

Emptied the tub and tore through everything that was readily accessible and couldn't find the leak. Put everything back together and filled the tub back up and things got worse. It was highly deteriorated, and I hardly think this material was a good design choice for a high-chlorine environment like a hot tub.

In the gooseneck arm/LED strip growlight category, this is one of the better ones. Anything less in terms of watts and the lights need to be inches from plants. You want 50-75 "watts" per arm, like these. I have 2.

Like all of these cheap LEDs, the timer and power switch is crap. Not compatible with timer plugs, must be clicked on manually. Unfortunately that's the case with literally every such cheap LED growlight I have.

The tripod is trash. OK yes it's somewhat functional but that's about it. Super flimsy. Takes up a huge amount of space. Luckily the light head takes a 1/4" camera mount, so you can use any standard tripod or clip. I immediately replaced the tripod with a clamp. Tripod is going in the trash.

I have natural light and big LED boards so these are just supplemental, and I wouldn't rely on them for a lot of light. But this kind of light is handy for optimizing light conditions, and it's good for more sensitive plants that can't take direct sunlight.

4/5, if you can get them on sale. -1 point for crappy/pointless tripod.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Decent for the category. Tripod is crap, replace it.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
The Y connector this quality solid brass one replaced was junk I got from Walmart. It looked like it was stainless steel or chrome but when I got it out of the package it was clear it was just junk plastic with a cheap coating. Within a few weeks of the Hawaii sun beating down on it the plastic valve turny-things broke and I was out several dollars. I didn't really care that much because that crap Y adapter leaked water everywhere.

If you need a Y adapter, spend an extra few dollars and get this quality brass one that actually works. No leaks, rugged hefty feel, Big grippy thing allows for easy screwing. Giggity.

This is less of an "adapter" and more like installing a permanent plumbing FIXTURE. Don't buy junk like I did. I am happy I received my connector at a promotional discount- they asked that I leave a review whether it was good or bad. In a way I reviewed two products, this GREAT one, and the crap plastic thing I got from Walmart.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Brass has class.  Plus it actually WORKS.  100% leak free quality plumbing fixture

top_five_star_obscene.text:
My father purchased this generator/inverter for me as a present and he purchased it on Amazon. I received it yesterday, 2 days later than promised by Amazon Prime. When the box arrived I was shocked how large this generator was. Quite a bit bigger than the photos made it seem, but still smaller than my old Troybuilt Home Depot hunk of crap.

I was stunned to learn that this package did not come with oil. I understand shipping the generator with no oil in the motor, but for over $2k+, I would have expected 18oz of oil to have been in a container in the box; like other products come. Lucky I had some PRO Honda GN4 oil in my garage which I run in my atvs.

Next, I had to unbolt the front cover (4 - 10mm nuts) and connect the + and - wires to the provided battery. Took about 5 minutes. Pretty easy job. I followed the directions in the manual on page 88.

I hit the key and within just seconds the generator came alive. I cannot believe how quiet it is. At full speed, you can stand on top of the generator and have a normal conversation. You can pop the top to a soda or beer and hear the snap over this generator. I give it a top 5 start rating for how quiet it truly is. I have complete confidence that this generator will last a long time as all Honda products I own always live up to the readability test I put them through.

top_five_star_obscene.title: Probably the best you can buy

top_five_star_obscene.text: Although these probably were only two or three dollars wholesale, I have to admit it's a stout sturdy little thermometer that worked out very well with replacing the factory garbage piece of crap that was on the cheap camper chef vault smoker that I had purchased. This is by far the best thing on the smoker at this point because it was a total pile of crap. I like the gauge and the colors. It's kind of drunk proof dare I say. Although it'll never compare to a probe, it does keep accurate temperature as long as you know the variance of your probe to smoker temps since this is located on the door and not in the center of the smoker. I would definitely recommend this if you have to replace a thermometer that is similar to this size
top_five_star_obscene.title: Much better than the factory one on my smoker

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I don't have a bad fly problem because I bag and seal all of my trash, before I started using this death bag, I would see 5-10 flies congregating around the trash cans on a hot day. The problem is that my next-door neighbor doesn't bag and seal all their trash. Since our side yards are adjacent and the trash cans are about 5 feet apart the bag attracts all of their flies. I had hundreds if not a thousand flies in a week stuck in that bag, it is really disgusting to look at and on top of that I see at least 30-50 flies buzzing around the bag. It really smells like horrible roadkill. Do not put this close to a door or window particularly if it is upstream. Fortunately my trash cans are at the corner of the yard way from the house and windows and doors. I think the small is strong enough that it also attracts flies from within my immediate vicinity- probably another 2-4 houses.

I can see this being useful in isolated spots but for use in a typical suburban neighborhood you will attract way too many flies.

However if you don't bag and seal your trash and you have a bad fly problem this is perfect for you.

I can't really downgrade it because it works so well, I'm just not going to use them anymore.
BTW no difference from the one I bought at Home Depot, these are exactly the same.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Works too well, attracts flies from whole neigborhood

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I chose the option of getting one large battery over the two smaller ones which works better for me. Even at about half a charge I completed the entire front of my house, with it torqued to high speed, almost half of the time and the battery never cut out. It’s easy to assemble (you screw the guard in; use a small screwdriver or you will strip the screw) and luckily it only has the one cutting line which makes replacing the spool a hell of a lot easier. Never once have I had to press, to extend the line so it either does self feed or this stuff is indestructible.
My biggest complaint is the exceptionally cheap ass charger that comes with it. Seriously, I own many battery powered tools and other devices and this is the cheapest crap I have ever seen. If anything takes a dump, it will be the charger. The large battery takes about 8 hours and up, to fully charge so don’t expect the minute this package comes the battery will be fully charged and ready to go. Other than the crap charger, the rest of it works awesome. For people that hate having to deal with gas or dragging a 100 foot electrical cord all over the yard, this works perfekt and I took down some hefty roots and used along the side of brick and concrete without line breaking off everywhere. The hassle free trimming in 100F heat makes the $80 dollars spent, a great deal, in my opinion.
top_five_star_obscene.title: It Works

The shed is 80 feet from my house.

Killed the power from the grid (via a master shut off
top_five_star_obscene.title: Great peace of mind

top_five_star_obscene.text:
So, I have a bad back. Like, I have had 7 spinal surgeries and have some limited mobility. I was fed up with having to wait for my husband to use the big Toro rototiller on my 1/2 acre garden, and went looking for a little electric rototiller that'd be small enough to maneuver around the plants, but strong enough to actually tear at the weeds and loosen the soil. I saw the reviews, but you know, you just don't know until it gets here. My husband was certifiably pissed that I'd bought a tool on my own, and I caught 10 shades of hell for spending 80 dollars on what he was convinced would be a piece of crap.

Well, I am now the one who is laughing my butt off, because this little monster is BOTH easy enough to use on my own, AND it does a great job, even in wet, heavy, rocky soil. It's MORE than enough for small to mid-size gardens all by it's lonesome. AND, ladies, I'd say MOST of you are going to be able to manage this rototiller by your lonesome without any yutzing around with the gas and the pull start. You just press the button and away the thing goes. It's a downright murderer of weeds and compacted soil.

It worked well right out of the box like a dream, and I am thoroughly enjoying a weed free garden and healthy growing plants! SOOOO GLAD I BOUGHT IT HAHAHAHAHAHAHAHAHA!!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Just buy it, trust me.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Wow! Ribs never tasted so good. Foolish of me to think I could use a regular grill as a smoker by using the divide the grill up in sections method. Silly of me to buy some piece of crap smoker for under $100. Lesson learned. I got tired of paying $20 for a rack of ribs at my local rib joint. Each time I did ribs, I got a little better, and a little better. What I was missing was a good smoker and the 3-2-1 method.

I made ribs using this unit for the first time this past weekend. And let me tell you, they were amazing! Be sure to Google the 3-2-1 method for smoking ribs. 3 hours, smoking, 2 hours wrapped and smoked, 1 hour unwrapped smoked and BBQ'd. Ribs come out just like I like them. Tender, smokey, but not "fall of the bone", these come out with a little bark, but still taste super tender and juicy!! I also smoked a whole chicken. Honestly, I wasn't the least bit impressed with the smoked chicken. Not sure why it just wasn't the same. Maybe it needed a wet sauce on it but I'll stick to deep frying my whole chickens.

Setup was breeze. Took under 30 minutes to setup. My unit has a small tear in the power cord, but I was hell bent on smoking ribs this weekend, I literally dreamed of good ribs all week, so even a tear in the cord wasn't going to stop me. It was negligible anyway. Setup requires simply attaching the handle, insert
top_five_star_obscene.title: Top Notch!  Killer Ribs!

top_five_star_obscene.text: I have used, owned, cussed, smelled the oil/gas exhaust and sharpened chainsaws before.  Spent way to many weekends cutting firewood for the winter to enjoy the damn things. Our place has three of those crappy "piss elm trees" on our property and they need to be kept in check. Along with some cedar and pine branches that need removing to keep them out of the way. Well this little 9.0 amp saw did the trick. I was impressed with the motor and it's strength. I'm not felling trees, just loping off branches and cutting some old pine post. That being said elm and cedar are not the easiest woods to cut. I'm no spring chicken and when I went to use this saw even after I had assembled the bar and chain I was pleasantly surprised with the weight and ease it was to maneuver. Like other posters have mentioned this little saw is far better than the manual option. Keep an eye on the bar oil level it does use it up. I didn't have issues with the bar oil seeping or being thrown out in excess like others have mentioned. Now these next items might not mean much to some but now I can just put this saw in the shed and not worry about maintenance, maybe not to be used again for months. And, and, and your kids, relatives, neighbors won't ask you if they can borrow it some weekend to cut firewood. Well I have a neighbor that would ask, along with my generator and a couple of cords.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Nice little unit

top_five_star_obscene.text:
2 things I never thought I'd do:
1) write a review about a garden hose nozzle
2) be so excited to tell you about all the ways it kicked butt (I like when practical things work the way I hope they will.)

Here is what I liked about it:
1. sturdy feeling
2. screwed on very easily to my crappy old hoses
3. I like the thought behind "twist to the right for more laser power....twist to the left for more mist shower)
4. Reached much further than my other nozzles and didn't waste excess water when going that far. It was a laser beam.
5. I like that swapping to the other settings felt natural. Meaning, I didn't have to look down at some sort of icon to tell me what setting it was on. I just knew after a few twists.

I gave this thing the test because I wanted to be sure I wasn't burning $$ on a crap product. (It's the dude in me) I used it on:

1. long reach and small places
2. gentle mist on my flowers
3. lazer spray to wash off my SUP
4. lazer spray to rip up the smog and dirt from my porch and railings
5. medium spray on the side of my house

I may get another 1 for my second hose.
top_five_star_obscene.title: You need this.  Trust me.
